Two-dimensional QBSDEs are technically much more challenging than the one-dimensional ones, and unless other assumptions are imposed, the well-posedness remains open; see the bibliographical notes in Subsection 7.4 of Zhang (2017).
title: Well-posedness theory for general multidimensional quadratic backward stochastic differential equations
background_latex:
A (multi-dimensional) quadratic backward stochastic differential equation (QBSDE) on a filtered probability space \((\Omega,\mathcal F,\mathbb F,\mathbb P)\) supporting a Brownian motion \(W\) seeks adapted processes \((Y_t,Z_t)_{t\in[0,T]}\) with values \(Y_t\in\mathbb R^m\), \(Z_t\in\mathbb R^{m\times d}\) such that
\[
Y_t = \xi + \int_t^T f\bigl(s,Y_s,Z_s\bigr)\,ds - \int_t^T Z_s\,dW_s,\qquad t\in[0,T],
\]
where \(\xi\in L^\infty(\mathcal F_T;\mathbb R^m)\) is a terminal condition and \(f\) is a progressively measurable generator. The term "quadratic" refers to growth of \(f\) like \(\|Z\|^2\) in \(Z\). For \(m=1\) (one-dimensional BSDEs), there are broad existence/uniqueness results under various structural assumptions even with quadratic growth in \(Z\). For \(m\ge 2\), the system is coupled and the analysis is substantially harder: even when \(\xi\) is bounded and \(f\) has quadratic growth, existence/uniqueness can fail and general sufficient conditions are not fully known.

In the paper’s portfolio-selection application with random market coefficients, an equilibrium strategy is characterized via a coupled system of two QBSDEs with quadratic generator; the authors note that known results for special structures (e.g., diagonally quadratic or triangular generators) do not apply to their specific system, and they prove well-posedness only by exploiting special structure and smallness conditions. This highlights the broader unresolved mathematical question of developing general well-posedness results for multi-dimensional QBSDEs beyond currently covered special cases.
problem_definition_latex:
Develop general existence and uniqueness conditions for solutions \((Y,Z)\) to multi-dimensional QBSDE systems (dimension \(m\ge 2\)) of the form
\[
Y_t = \xi + \int_t^T f\bigl(s,Y_s,Z_s\bigr)\,ds - \int_t^T Z_s\,dW_s,
\]
when the generator \(f\) has quadratic growth in \(Z\) and the components of \(Y\) are coupled (i.e., the system is genuinely multi-dimensional), in settings not covered by existing diagonally quadratic or triangular structural assumptions, and without restricting to the special “pure linear-quadratic” forms used in specific applications.

open_problem_quote: Two-dimensional QBSDEs are technically much more challenging than the one-dimensional ones, and unless other assumptions are imposed, the well-posedness remains open; see the bibliographical notes in Subsection 7.4 of Zhang (2017).
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement concerns general existence/uniqueness theory for a broad class of stochastic integral equations rather than a finite combinatorial claim; automated counterexample search would require specifying a restricted parametric family of generators and terminal conditions and numerically detecting nonexistence/nonuniqueness, which is not a reliable, decidable pipeline.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Evaluating candidate theoretical conditions or proofs is not readily automatable via a computable fitness signal. While one could test sufficient conditions on simulated instances, that does not validate correctness of a general well-posedness theorem, so an evolutionary coding loop would have limited ability to make rigorous progress.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
